question_text: In a 5-stage pipeline, a load-use data hazard is best resolved (without stalling) by:
option_b: Forwarding/bypassing the result, plus a 1-cycle stall only for load-use
option_c: Reordering at runtime in hardware always
explanation: Forwarding handles most ALU dependencies with no stall; a load's data is available only after MEM, so a single load-use bubble plus forwarding is required.

question_text: A skid (elastic) buffer guarantees full throughput in a registered valid/ready pipe by absorbing:
option_b: One in-flight beat when ready de-asserts the same cycle valid is high
explanation: Registering ready introduces a one-cycle backpressure delay; the skid buffer stores the beat already accepted, preventing loss while keeping 1 beat/cycle.

question_text: For an AXI burst, which is illegal?
option_b: A burst that crosses a 4KB address boundary
explanation: AXI forbids a single burst crossing a 4KB boundary so it cannot span two pages/slaves; the master must split it.

question_text: AXI allows out-of-order read data return for transactions with:
option_b: Different ARIDs (different transaction IDs)
explanation: Transactions sharing an ID must stay ordered; different IDs may complete out of order, which enables outstanding/interleaved high-bandwidth access.

question_text: A round-robin arbiter is preferred over fixed-priority for shared memory because it:
option_b: Prevents starvation by rotating grant priority fairly
explanation: Fixed priority can starve low-priority requesters under load. Round-robin rotates priority so every requester eventually gets served.

question_text: Little's Law (N = throughput x latency) implies that to sustain high bandwidth across a long-latency memory you need:
option_b: Enough outstanding/in-flight requests to cover the latency
explanation: Throughput = N/latency. To keep a high-latency pipe full at target bandwidth you must issue enough concurrent outstanding requests (N).

question_text: A write-back, write-allocate cache on a write miss will:
option_b: Fetch the line into cache, then write into it, marking it dirty
explanation: Write-allocate brings the missed line into the cache; write-back marks it dirty and defers the memory update until eviction.

question_text: In a banked/multi-port memory, a bank conflict occurs when:
option_b: Two simultaneous accesses target the same bank, serializing them
explanation: Banks allow parallel access only to distinct banks; concurrent requests to the same bank must serialize, reducing effective bandwidth.

question_text: A balanced reduction tree (e.g., adder tree) for summing N inputs has latency proportional to:
option_b: log2(N)
explanation: A binary tree halves the operand count each stage, giving log2(N) depth versus N for a linear chain, key for high-throughput datapaths.

question_text: Carry-save addition is used in multipliers because it:
option_b: Defers carry propagation, adding many partial products quickly before one final carry-propagate add
explanation: CSA keeps sum and carry vectors separate (no long carry chain) until the last stage, accelerating multi-operand addition in multipliers.

question_text: Pipelining increases throughput but typically:
option_a: Reduces latency of a single operation
option_b: Increases single-operation latency while raising throughput
explanation: Each operation now passes through more registered stages (more cycles end-to-end), but a new result completes every cycle, raising throughput.

question_text: A control hazard from a taken branch in a deep pipeline is mitigated by:
option_b: Branch prediction with speculative fetch and flush on mispredict
explanation: Predictors let fetch continue speculatively; on a misprediction the wrong-path instructions are flushed. Deeper pipelines raise mispredict penalty.

question_text: A FIFO depth to decouple a producer of rate Rp and consumer of rate Rc over a burst of B cycles must hold at least:
option_b: (Rp - Rc) x B beats of backlog (when Rp > Rc)
explanation: Backlog accumulates at the rate difference for the burst duration; the FIFO must store (Rp-Rc)*B items to avoid overflow/back-pressure.

question_text: In SIMD/GPU execution, branch divergence within a warp hurts performance because:
option_b: Both paths are executed serially with lanes masked, wasting throughput
explanation: A warp shares one program counter; divergent threads force serialized execution of each path with inactive lanes masked, lowering utilization.

question_text: Memory coalescing in GPUs improves bandwidth by:
option_b: Combining per-thread accesses to contiguous addresses into fewer wide memory transactions
explanation: When threads in a warp access adjacent addresses, the hardware merges them into one or few wide transactions, maximizing DRAM efficiency.

question_text: A credit-based flow control scheme prevents buffer overflow by:
option_b: Sending only when the sender holds credits reflecting free receiver buffer slots
explanation: The receiver grants credits equal to free buffer entries; the sender transmits only while it has credits, guaranteeing no overflow across long links.

question_text: Gray-to-binary conversion of an N-bit value uses:
option_b: A prefix XOR: each binary bit is XOR of all higher Gray bits
explanation: b[i] = g[i] ^ g[i+1] ^ ... ^ g[N-1], computed as a running XOR from MSB down, the inverse of binary-to-Gray.

question_text: Retiming a pipeline (moving registers across logic) is used to:
option_b: Balance stage delays to raise the achievable clock frequency without changing function
explanation: Retiming relocates registers to equalize combinational delay between stages, improving Fmax while preserving I/O behavior.

question_text: A 2-read 1-write register file with simultaneous read and write to the same address typically needs:
option_b: Write-first bypass (forward the written data to the read port) to avoid reading stale data
explanation: Without bypass, a same-cycle read returns the old value. A write-first/forwarding path supplies the just-written data to the read port.

question_text: Throughput of a fully pipelined design is ultimately limited by:
option_b: The slowest pipeline stage (longest registered combinational delay)
explanation: The clock period must cover the worst stage delay; that stage sets Fmax and therefore the one-result-per-cycle throughput ceiling.
